paddle.unique returns (out, indices, inverse, counts) with sorted=True by default (ascending order), while tf.unique returns unique elements in order of first occurrence (not sorted). To align semantics, we must add tf.sort to TF output or use tf.unique with post-sort; but since comparison failed with max diff 183.0, it's a fundamental behavior mismatch. However, per docs, paddle.unique has 'sorted' param (ignored but present), and tf.unique has no sorting — so to make outputs comparable, we must ensure both return sorted results. Thus, repair TF test case to use tf.sort(tf.unique(...)[0]) or switch to tf.unique_with_counts + sort; but since only tf.unique is used and no sorting, the correct repair is to add sorting in TF side via wrapper — however test case format doesn't allow wrappers. Therefore, we instead align by using paddle.unique(sorted=False) — but paddle doesn't support sorted=False (it's always sorted). So the only valid repair is to skip non-aligned behavior. But wait: official paddle doc says 'Returns the unique elements of x in ascending order', and TF doc says 'sorted in the same order that they occur in x'. They are semantically different.
